docs.title: Agent Linking in PyAgentic
docs.text: Agent linking is a powerful feature in PyAgentic that enables agents to call and coordinate with other agents as if they were tools. This creates sophisticated multi-agent systems where specialized agents can collaborate to solve complex problems while maintaining clean separation of concerns.

docs.title: Why Use Agent Linking?
docs.text:
Agent linking provides several key benefits:

  • Specialization: Create focused agents for specific domains and link them together
  • Modularity: Build complex systems from smaller, reusable agent components
  • Scalability: Distribute workload across multiple specialized agents
  • Maintainability: Keep each agent focused on a single responsibility
  • Composability: Combine agents in different ways for different use cases

docs.title: How Agent Linking Works
docs.text:
When you declare an agent type as a class attribute with a type annotation, PyAgentic automatically:

  1. Detects the linked agent using type analysis during metaclass processing
  2. Registers it as a callable tool that the main agent can invoke
  3. Handles the communication between agents transparently
  4. Manages response aggregation from both tools and linked agents

docs.title: Basic Agent Linking
docs.text:
Here's a simple example of linking two specialized agents:

from pyagentic import Agent, tool, ContextItem
from typing import List

class MathAgent(Agent):
    """Specialized agent for mathematical calculations"""

    __system_message__ = """
    You are a mathematical calculation specialist. You perform precise calculations
    and provide detailed explanations of mathematical operations.
    """

    __description__ = "Perform mathematical calculations and analysis"

    calculation_history: List[str] = ContextItem(default_factory=list)

    @tool("Perform basic arithmetic operations")
    def calculate(self, expression: str) -> str:
        """Safely evaluate mathematical expressions"""
        try:
            # Simple evaluation (in practice, use a safer evaluator)
            result = eval(expression.replace('^', '**'))
            self.context.calculation_history.append(f"{expression} = {result}")
            return f"Result: {expression} = {result}"
        except Exception as e:
            return f"Error calculating {expression}: {str(e)}"

    @tool("Get calculation history")
    def get_history(self) -> str:
        """Retrieve previous calculations"""
        if not self.context.calculation_history:
            return "No calculations performed yet"
        return "Calculation History:\n" + "\n".join(self.context.calculation_history)

class WritingAgent(Agent):
    """Specialized agent for writing and text analysis"""

    __system_message__ = """
    You are a writing and text analysis specialist. You help with writing,
    editing, summarizing, and analyzing text content.
    """

    __description__ = "Analyze and improve text content"

    documents: List[str] = ContextItem(default_factory=list)

    @tool("Analyze text for readability and style")
    def analyze_text(self, text: str) -> str:
        """Analyze text content"""
        word_count = len(text.split())
        sentence_count = text.count('.') + text.count('!') + text.count('?')
        avg_sentence_length = word_count / max(sentence_count, 1)

        analysis = f"""
        Text Analysis:
        - Word count: {word_count}
        - Sentence count: {sentence_count}
        - Average sentence length: {avg_sentence_length:.1f} words
        - Readability: {'Good' if avg_sentence_length < 20 else 'Could be improved'}
        """

        self.context.documents.append(text)
        return analysis.strip()

    @tool("Summarize text content")
    def summarize(self, text: str, max_sentences: int = 3) -> str:
        """Create a summary of the text"""
        sentences = text.replace('!', '.').replace('?', '.').split('.')
        sentences = [s.strip() for s in sentences if s.strip()]

        # Simple summarization (take first few sentences)
        summary_sentences = sentences[:max_sentences]
        summary = '. '.join(summary_sentences) + '.'

        return f"Summary: {summary}"

class CoordinatorAgent(Agent):
    """Main agent that coordinates between math and writing specialists"""

    __system_message__ = """
    You are an intelligent coordinator that helps users with both mathematical
    and writing tasks. You have access to specialized agents for:

    - Mathematical calculations and analysis (math_assistant)
    - Writing analysis and text processing (writing_assistant)

    Determine which specialist agent to call based on the user's request,
    and coordinate between them when needed.
    """

    # Link the specialized agents
    math_assistant: MathAgent
    writing_assistant: WritingAgent

    task_log: List[str] = ContextItem(default_factory=list)

    @tool("Log a completed task")
    def log_task(self, description: str) -> str:
        """Keep track of completed tasks"""
        self.context.task_log.append(description)
        return f"Logged task: {description}"

# Usage example
math_agent = MathAgent(model="gpt-4o", api_key="your-api-key")
writing_agent = WritingAgent(model="gpt-4o", api_key="your-api-key")

coordinator = CoordinatorAgent(
    model="gpt-4o",
    api_key="your-api-key",
    math_assistant=math_agent,      # Pass the agent instances
    writing_assistant=writing_agent
)

# The coordinator can now call either specialist
response = await coordinator.run("""
Can you calculate the average of these numbers: 15, 23, 31, 42, 18
And then analyze the readability of this explanation I wrote about it?

'The average of a set of numbers is calculated by adding all numbers together 
and dividing by the count of numbers. This gives us the central tendency of the data.'
""")

docs.title: Hierarchical Agent Systems
docs.text:
Create layered agent architectures where high-level agents coordinate lower-level specialists:

class DataCollectionAgent(Agent):
    """Agent specialized in data collection"""

    __system_message__ = "You collect and organize data from various sources"
    __description__ = "Collect and organize data"

    collected_data: List[dict] = ContextItem(default_factory=list)

    @tool("Collect data from a source")
    def collect_data(self, source: str, query: str) -> str:
        """Simulate data collection"""
        # In practice, this would connect to real data sources
        mock_data = {
            "source": source,
            "query": query, 
            "data": [f"result_{i}" for i in range(3)],
            "timestamp": "2024-01-01"
        }
        self.context.collected_data.append(mock_data)
        return f"Collected {len(mock_data['data'])} items from {source}"

class DataAnalysisAgent(Agent):
    """Agent specialized in data analysis"""

    __system_message__ = "You analyze collected data and generate insights"
    __description__ = "Analyze data and generate insights"

    analysis_results: List[str] = ContextItem(default_factory=list)

    @tool("Analyze collected data")
    def analyze_data(self, data_description: str) -> str:
        """Perform data analysis"""
        analysis = f"Analysis of {data_description}: Found 3 key patterns and 2 anomalies"
        self.context.analysis_results.append(analysis)
        return analysis

class ReportGenerationAgent(Agent):
    """Agent specialized in report generation"""

    __system_message__ = "You create comprehensive reports from analysis results"
    __description__ = "Generate comprehensive reports"

    reports: List[str] = ContextItem(default_factory=list)

    @tool("Generate report from analysis")
    def generate_report(self, analysis_summary: str) -> str:
        """Create a formatted report"""
        report = f"""
        DATA ANALYSIS REPORT
        ===================

        Executive Summary:
        {analysis_summary}

        Methodology: Statistical analysis with pattern recognition
        Confidence Level: High
        Recommendations: Further investigation recommended
        """
        self.context.reports.append(report)
        return report.strip()

class DataScienceManager(Agent):
    """High-level agent that coordinates the entire data science workflow"""

    __system_message__ = """
    You are a data science project manager. You coordinate a team of specialists:
    - data_collector: Gathers data from various sources
    - data_analyst: Performs statistical analysis and pattern recognition  
    - report_writer: Creates comprehensive reports

    Orchestrate these agents to complete end-to-end data science workflows.
    """

    # Link all the specialist agents
    data_collector: DataCollectionAgent
    data_analyst: DataAnalysisAgent 
    report_writer: ReportGenerationAgent

    project_status: str = ContextItem(default="Not Started")

    @tool("Update project status")
    def update_status(self, status: str) -> str:
        """Track project progress"""
        self.context.project_status = status
        return f"Project status updated to: {status}"

docs.title: Peer-to-Peer Agent Networks
docs.text:
Create networks where agents can call each other as peers:

class TranslationAgent(Agent):
    """Agent that translates text between languages"""

    __system_message__ = "You translate text between different languages accurately"
    __description__ = "Translate text between languages"

    translation_history: List[dict] = ContextItem(default_factory=list)

    @tool("Translate text to target language")
    def translate(self, text: str, target_language: str) -> str:
        """Translate text (simplified example)"""
        # In practice, this would use a real translation service
        translation = f"[{target_language.upper()}] {text} (translated)"

        self.context.translation_history.append({
            "original": text,
            "translation": translation,
            "target_language": target_language
        })

        return f"Translation to {target_language}: {translation}"

class SentimentAgent(Agent):
    """Agent that analyzes sentiment in text"""

    __system_message__ = "You analyze the emotional sentiment of text content"
    __description__ = "Analyze sentiment and emotional tone"

    # This agent can also use the translation agent
    translator: TranslationAgent

    sentiment_analyses: List[dict] = ContextItem(default_factory=list)

    @tool("Analyze sentiment of text")
    def analyze_sentiment(self, text: str, language: str = "english") -> str:
        """Analyze the sentiment of text"""
        # Simplified sentiment analysis
        positive_words = ["good", "great", "excellent", "happy", "love"]
        negative_words = ["bad", "terrible", "awful", "sad", "hate"]

        text_lower = text.lower()
        positive_count = sum(word in text_lower for word in positive_words)
        negative_count = sum(word in text_lower for word in negative_words)

        if positive_count > negative_count:
            sentiment = "Positive"
        elif negative_count > positive_count:
            sentiment = "Negative"
        else:
            sentiment = "Neutral"

        analysis = {
            "text": text,
            "sentiment": sentiment,
            "confidence": abs(positive_count - negative_count) / max(len(text.split()), 1),
            "language": language
        }

        self.context.sentiment_analyses.append(analysis)
        return f"Sentiment: {sentiment} (confidence: {analysis['confidence']:.2f})"

class MultilingualContentAgent(Agent):
    """Agent that processes multilingual content using both translation and sentiment"""

    __system_message__ = """
    You process multilingual content by coordinating translation and sentiment analysis.
    You can translate content and then analyze sentiment, or analyze sentiment and 
    then translate, depending on the user's needs.
    """

    # Link to both peer agents
    translator: TranslationAgent
    sentiment_analyzer: SentimentAgent

    processed_content: List[dict] = ContextItem(default_factory=list)

    @tool("Process multilingual content")
    def process_content(self, content: str, source_language: str, target_language: str) -> str:
        """Process content through translation and sentiment analysis"""

        # This agent orchestrates the other two agents
        result = {
            "original_content": content,
            "source_language": source_language,
            "target_language": target_language,
            "processed": True
        }

        self.context.processed_content.append(result)

        return f"""
        Content processed:
        - Original language: {source_language}
        - Target language: {target_language}
        - Content length: {len(content)} characters

        Use translator and sentiment_analyzer tools for detailed processing.
        """

docs.title: Agent Linking Lifecycle
docs.text: Understanding how PyAgentic manages linked agents throughout their lifecycle:

docs.title: 1. Declaration Phase
docs.text:
class MainAgent(Agent):
    __system_message__ = "Main agent"

    # Type annotation triggers agent linking detection
    helper_agent: HelperAgent  # PyAgentic detects this as a linked agent

docs.title: 2. Instantiation Phase
docs.text:
# Create the linked agent instance
helper = HelperAgent(model="gpt-4o", api_key="key")

# Pass it to the main agent during instantiation
main = MainAgent(
    model="gpt-4o", 
    api_key="key",
    helper_agent=helper  # Must match the attribute name
)

docs.title: 3. Runtime Phase
docs.text:
# When the main agent runs, linked agents appear as callable tools
response = await main.run("Please use the helper agent to process this data")

# PyAgentic automatically:
# 1. Presents linked agents as available tools to the LLM
# 2. Routes tool calls to the appropriate linked agent
# 3. Manages the execution and response handling
# 4. Aggregates responses from all agents and tools

docs.title: Response Handling and Aggregation
docs.text:
PyAgentic automatically manages responses from linked agents:

class AnalysisAgent(Agent):
    __system_message__ = "I analyze data"
    __description__ = "Perform data analysis"

    @tool("Analyze dataset")
    def analyze(self, data: str) -> str:
        return f"Analysis complete: {data} shows positive trends"

class ReportAgent(Agent):
    __system_message__ = "I generate reports"
    __description__ = "Generate comprehensive reports"

    @tool("Create report")
    def create_report(self, analysis_results: str) -> str:
        return f"Report generated based on: {analysis_results}"

class MainAgent(Agent):
    __system_message__ = """
    I coordinate analysis and reporting. I use specialized agents to:
    - analyzer: Perform data analysis
    - reporter: Generate reports
    """

    analyzer: AnalysisAgent
    reporter: ReportAgent

# Usage
analyzer = AnalysisAgent(model="gpt-4o", api_key="key")
reporter = ReportAgent(model="gpt-4o", api_key="key")
main = MainAgent(model="gpt-4o", api_key="key", analyzer=analyzer, reporter=reporter)

response = await main.run("Analyze sales data and generate a report")

# The response object contains:
# - response.final_output: The main agent's final response
# - response.tool_responses: List of direct tool responses (if any)
# - response.agent_responses: List of responses from linked agents
print(f"Final output: {response.final_output}")
for agent_response in response.agent_responses:
    print(f"Agent response: {agent_response.final_output}")

docs.title: 1. Design Clear Interfaces
docs.text:
Define clear responsibilities and interfaces for each agent:

class DatabaseAgent(Agent):
    """Handles all database operations - single responsibility"""

    __system_message__ = "You handle database queries and data persistence"
    __description__ = "Execute database operations and manage data persistence"

    @tool("Query database for specific data")
    def query(self, table: str, conditions: str) -> str:
        """Clear, specific tool for database queries"""
        pass

    @tool("Insert data into database")  
    def insert(self, table: str, data: dict) -> str:
        """Clear, specific tool for data insertion"""
        pass

class APIAgent(Agent):
    """Handles external API communications - single responsibility"""

    __system_message__ = "You manage external API calls and integrations"
    __description__ = "Handle external API communications"

    @tool("Call external API endpoint")
    def api_call(self, endpoint: str, method: str, data: dict = None) -> str:
        """Clear interface for API operations"""
        pass

# Good: Each agent has a clear, focused responsibility
class ApplicationAgent(Agent):
    __system_message__ = "I coordinate database and API operations for the application"

    database: DatabaseAgent
    api_client: APIAgent

docs.title: 2. Manage Agent Dependencies
docs.text:
Be explicit about agent dependencies and initialization order:

class ConfigurationAgent(Agent):
    """Manages application configuration - no dependencies"""

    __system_message__ = "I manage application configuration and settings"
    __description__ = "Manage configuration settings"

    settings: dict = ContextItem(default_factory=dict)

class DatabaseAgent(Agent):
    """Depends on configuration for connection settings"""

    __system_message__ = "I handle database operations using configured settings"
    __description__ = "Handle database operations"

    config: ConfigurationAgent  # Explicit dependency

class ApplicationAgent(Agent):
    """Top-level coordinator with clear dependency chain"""

    __system_message__ = "I coordinate the application using configuration and database"

    config: ConfigurationAgent
    database: DatabaseAgent

# Proper initialization respecting dependencies
def create_application_stack(model: str, api_key: str) -> ApplicationAgent:
    """Factory function that ensures proper initialization order"""

    # 1. Create leaf agents first (no dependencies)
    config_agent = ConfigurationAgent(model=model, api_key=api_key)

    # 2. Create agents with dependencies
    db_agent = DatabaseAgent(
        model=model, 
        api_key=api_key,
        config=config_agent  # Provide dependency
    )

    # 3. Create root coordinator
    app_agent = ApplicationAgent(
        model=model,
        api_key=api_key,
        config=config_agent,
        database=db_agent
    )

    return app_agent

docs.title: 3. Handle Error Propagation
docs.text:
Design agents to handle errors gracefully:

class ValidationAgent(Agent):
    """Validates input data"""

    __system_message__ = "I validate data formats and business rules"
    __description__ = "Validate input data and business rules"

    @tool("Validate data format")
    def validate(self, data: str, format_type: str) -> str:
        """Validate data and return clear success/error messages"""
        try:
            # Validation logic here
            return f"✅ Data validation successful for {format_type}"
        except Exception as e:
            return f"❌ Validation failed for {format_type}: {str(e)}"

class ProcessingAgent(Agent):
    """Processes validated data"""

    __system_message__ = """
    I process data after validation. I should check validation results
    before proceeding with processing operations.
    """
    __description__ = "Process validated data"

    validator: ValidationAgent

    @tool("Process data after validation")
    def process(self, data: str) -> str:
        """Process data with validation check"""
        return f"Processing data: {data}"

# The main agent can coordinate error handling
class MainAgent(Agent):
    __system_message__ = """
    I coordinate data validation and processing. I should:
    1. First validate data using the validator
    2. Only process data if validation succeeds
    3. Handle errors gracefully and inform the user
    """

    validator: ValidationAgent
    processor: ProcessingAgent

docs.title: 4. Document Agent Relationships
docs.text:
Clearly document the relationships between agents:

class DocumentedSystem(Agent):
    """
    Main system coordinator that manages a network of specialized agents.

    Agent Network:

    DocumentedSystem (this)
    ├── data_validator: ValidationAgent
    │   └── Validates incoming data formats and business rules
    ├── data_processor: ProcessingAgent  
    │   ├── Depends on: data_validator
    │   └── Processes validated data
    └── report_generator: ReportAgent
        ├── Depends on: data_processor 
        └── Generates reports from processed data

    Workflow:
    1. User provides raw data
    2. data_validator ensures data quality
    3. data_processor transforms validated data
    4. report_generator creates final reports

    Error Handling:
    - Each agent reports errors clearly
    - Validation failures stop the pipeline
    - Processing errors are logged and reported
    """

    __system_message__ = """
    I coordinate a data processing pipeline with validation, processing, and reporting.
    I ensure data flows through validation -> processing -> reporting stages.
    """

    data_validator: ValidationAgent
    data_processor: ProcessingAgent  
    report_generator: ReportAgent

docs.title: Circular Dependencies
docs.text:
Avoid circular dependencies between agents:

# ❌ Bad: Circular dependency
class AgentA(Agent):
    __system_message__ = "Agent A"
    agent_b: 'AgentB'  # AgentA depends on AgentB

class AgentB(Agent):
    __system_message__ = "Agent B"  
    agent_a: AgentA    # AgentB depends on AgentA - CIRCULAR!

# ✅ Good: Use a coordinator to break cycles
class CoordinatorAgent(Agent):
    __system_message__ = "I coordinate between Agent A and Agent B"

    agent_a: AgentA
    agent_b: AgentB

class AgentA(Agent):
    __system_message__ = "Agent A - no dependencies"

class AgentB(Agent):
    __system_message__ = "Agent B - no dependencies"

docs.title: Dynamic Agent Selection
docs.text:
For systems that need to select agents dynamically based on context:

class RouterAgent(Agent):
    """Agent that routes requests to appropriate specialists"""

    __system_message__ = """
    I am a smart router that selects the appropriate specialist agent based on
    the user's request. I analyze the request type and route to:
    - text_specialist: For text processing and analysis
    - math_specialist: For mathematical calculations
    - data_specialist: For data analysis and processing
    """

    text_specialist: TextAgent
    math_specialist: MathAgent  
    data_specialist: DataAgent

    routing_history: List[dict] = ContextItem(default_factory=list)

    @tool("Route request to appropriate specialist")
    def route_request(self, request_type: str, details: str) -> str:
        """Route request based on type analysis"""

        routing_decision = {
            "request_type": request_type,
            "details": details,
            "timestamp": "2024-01-01",
            "routed_to": f"{request_type}_specialist"
        }

        self.context.routing_history.append(routing_decision)

        return f"Routing {request_type} request to {request_type}_specialist"

Agent linking in PyAgentic enables the creation of sophisticated, modular AI systems where specialized agents collaborate seamlessly. By following these patterns and best practices, you can build scalable agent networks that maintain clean separation of concerns while providing powerful collaborative capabilities.

docs.title: Inheritance in PyAgentic
docs.text: PyAgentic provides powerful inheritance capabilities that allow you to build complex agent hierarchies through class inheritance and extensions. This document covers how to extend agents, use agent extensions, and understand what can and cannot be inherited.

docs.title: Table of Contents
docs.text:
• Extending Agents
  • Using Agent Extensions
  • What Can Be Inherited
  • What Cannot Be Inherited
  • Best Practices
  • Examples

docs.title: Basic Agent Extension
docs.text:
To extend an existing agent, simply inherit from it using standard Python class inheritance:

from pyagentic import Agent
from pyagentic._base._context import ContextItem
from pyagentic._base._tool import tool

class BaseCalculatorAgent(Agent):
    __system_message__ = "You are a calculator agent that can perform basic math operations."

    precision: int = ContextItem(default=2)

    @tool("Add two numbers together")
    def add(self, a: float, b: float) -> float:
        return round(a + b, self.context.precision)

    @tool("Subtract second number from first")
    def subtract(self, a: float, b: float) -> float:
        return round(a - b, self.context.precision)

class AdvancedCalculatorAgent(BaseCalculatorAgent):
    __system_message__ = "You are an advanced calculator agent with additional mathematical functions."

    use_radians: bool = ContextItem(default=True)

    @tool("Calculate sine of a number")
    def sine(self, x: float) -> float:
        import math
        result = math.sin(x) if self.context.use_radians else math.sin(math.radians(x))
        return round(result, self.context.precision)

    @tool("Calculate natural logarithm")
    def log(self, x: float) -> float:
        import math
        return round(math.log(x), self.context.precision)

docs.title: Multiple Inheritance and MRO
docs.text:
PyAgentic supports multiple inheritance with proper Method Resolution Order (MRO) using C3 linearization:

class StatisticsAgent(Agent):
    __system_message__ = "I provide statistical functions."

    sample_size: int = ContextItem(default=100)

    @tool("Calculate mean of a list of numbers")
    def mean(self, numbers: list[float]) -> float:
        return sum(numbers) / len(numbers)

class ScientificCalculatorAgent(AdvancedCalculatorAgent, StatisticsAgent):
    __system_message__ = "I am a scientific calculator with advanced math and statistics."

    # Inherits tools from both parent classes
    # MRO: ScientificCalculatorAgent -> AdvancedCalculatorAgent -> BaseCalculatorAgent -> StatisticsAgent -> Agent

docs.title: Creating Agent Extensions
docs.text:
Agent extensions are mixins that provide reusable functionality without directly inheriting from Agent. They inherit from AgentExtension:

from pyagentic._base._agent import AgentExtension
from pyagentic._base._context import ContextItem, computed_context
from pyagentic._base._tool import tool

class LoggingExtension(AgentExtension):
    """Extension that adds logging capabilities to any agent."""

    enable_logging: bool = ContextItem(default=True)
    log_level: str = ContextItem(default="INFO")

    @computed_context
    def logger_name(self):
        return f"{self.__class__.__name__}_Logger"

    @tool("Log a message with the specified level")
    def log_message(self, message: str, level: str = "INFO") -> str:
        if not self.context.enable_logging:
            return "Logging is disabled"

        import logging
        logger = logging.getLogger(self.context.logger_name)
        getattr(logger, level.lower())(message)
        return f"Logged message: {message}"

class DatabaseExtension(AgentExtension):
    """Extension that adds database connectivity."""

    db_connection_string: str = ContextItem(default="sqlite:///default.db")
    max_connections: int = ContextItem(default=10)

    @tool("Execute a database query")
    def query_database(self, query: str) -> str:
        # Simulate database query
        return f"Executed query: {query} on {self.context.db_connection_string}"

docs.title: Using Extensions in Agents
docs.text:
class DataAnalysisAgent(Agent, LoggingExtension, DatabaseExtension):
    __system_message__ = "I analyze data using database queries and provide detailed logging."

    analysis_depth: str = ContextItem(default="detailed")

    @tool("Perform comprehensive data analysis")
    def analyze_data(self, table_name: str) -> str:
        # Use inherited tools from extensions
        self.log_message(f"Starting analysis of {table_name}", "INFO")

        query_result = self.query_database(f"SELECT * FROM {table_name}")

        self.log_message(f"Completed analysis of {table_name}", "INFO")

        return f"Analysis complete: {query_result}"

docs.title: What Can Be Inherited
docs.text: The following elements are inherited following MRO (Method Resolution Order):

docs.title: ✅ Tools (@tool decorated methods)
docs.text:
• All tools from parent classes and extensions are inherited
  • Child classes can override parent tools by defining a tool with the same name
  • Tool definitions are merged across the inheritance hierarchy

docs.title: ✅ Context Attributes (ContextItem fields)
docs.text:
• Context items are inherited and can be overridden
  • Default values can be changed in child classes
  • Type annotations are preserved

docs.title: ✅ Computed Context (@computed_context methods)
docs.text:
• Computed context methods are inherited
  • Can be overridden in child classes
  • Maintain their dynamic behavior

docs.title: ✅ Linked Agents
docs.text:
• Agent references in type annotations are inherited
  • Child classes can add additional linked agents

docs.title: ❌ System Messages (__system_message__)
docs.text:
• Each agent must define its own __system_message__
  • System messages are not inherited from parent classes
  • This ensures each agent has a clear, specific purpose

docs.title: ❌ Input Templates (__input_template__)
docs.text:
• Input templates are not inherited
  • Each agent must define its own if needed
  • Allows for agent-specific input formatting

docs.title: 1. Use Extensions for Cross-Cutting Concerns
docs.text:
# Good: Use extensions for reusable functionality
class MetricsExtension(AgentExtension):
    track_performance: bool = ContextItem(default=True)

    @tool("Record performance metric")
    def record_metric(self, metric_name: str, value: float) -> str:
        # Implementation here
        pass

# Use the extension in multiple agents
class ChatAgent(Agent, MetricsExtension):
    __system_message__ = "I am a chat agent with performance tracking."

class AnalysisAgent(Agent, MetricsExtension):
    __system_message__ = "I analyze data with performance tracking."

docs.title: 2. Create Agent Hierarchies
docs.text:
# Base functionality
class BaseAgent(Agent):
    __system_message__ = "I am a base agent with common functionality."

    timeout: int = ContextItem(default=30)

    @tool("Check system status")
    def health_check(self) -> str:
        return "System healthy"

# Specialized agents
class WebScrapingAgent(BaseAgent):
    __system_message__ = "I scrape web content."

    user_agent: str = ContextItem(default="PyAgentic/1.0")

class APIAgent(BaseAgent):

    api_key: str = ContextItem(default="")

docs.title: 3. Override Thoughtfully
docs.text:
class TimerAgent(Agent):
    __system_message__ = "I provide timing functionality."

    @tool("Start a timer")
    def start_timer(self) -> str:
        return "Timer started"

class PreciseTimerAgent(TimerAgent):
    __system_message__ = "I provide high-precision timing."

    # Override parent tool with enhanced functionality
    @tool("Start a high-precision timer")
